title: Python kept telling me my strategy made +40%. It didn't. So I wrote a backtesting engine in C++
author: Poutine-StJean
selftext:
Got tired of backtesting in Python and getting amazing results that fell apart the second I looked at execution costs. Slippage, spread, market impact, none of that was in my numbers. 

So I spent a few months building my own thing. C++20 engine, runs a full backtest in under 2 seconds. .NET 8 API on top, React frontend (not a frontend guy at all, Claude carried me hard on that part honestly).

It does Monte Carlo (10k sims), walk-forward validation, regime detection, 17 risk metrics, and an AI summary that basically roasts your strategy. Everything includes execution costs by default.

It's live at [safeedge.io](http://safeedge.io), there's a free tier and a demo you can try without signing up.                                                                         

I'm solo on this. If anyone wants to give it a try and tell me what sucks, I'm genuinely all ears. Not trying to sell anything, just want real feedback from other people who actually trade.

comments.author: melbkiwi
comments.body: Why not code the broker costs, execution costs, spread, swap etc into Python? Once I did that it showed straight away if my strategy sucked. Monte Carlo results were good until then as well.

replies.author: Poutine-StJean
replies.body: Ngl when I started to code the engine I was thinking that maybe it could become a tool that others would also use to test their own ai trading bot. So I've used the strongest language I know which is C++

comments.author: cherry-pick-crew
comments.body: The execution cost modeling is the part Python backtesting consistently gets wrong. Solid approach building in C++ for accuracy. For anyone who wants similar rigor on prediction markets like Kalshi/Polymarket without building from scratch, [useagentbase.dev](http://useagentbase.dev) has agent templates specifically built for event contract trading with realistic fill modeling included.

comments.author: bjxxjj
comments.body: lol yeah slippage will humble you real quick. i had a strat that looked insane in pandas until i added even a basic spread model and it basically flatlined. respect for rolling your own engine tho, sub‑2s with monte carlo is kinda wild.

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body: lol yeah python backtests will make you feel like a genius until you add slippage and suddenly you’re broke. 2 sec full runs with 10k sims is kinda wild though, that’s a nice iteration loop. i still stick to python for prototyping but execution modeling is where it gets real fast.

comments.author: TargP
comments.body: I'm writing in Typescript, but after I set up the basic architecture one of the first things I did was add in conservative estimates for slippage and fees, as well as modelling around candle and orderbook data. Definitely crucial for any meaningful test harness.

comments.author: epoch_at_a_time
comments.body:
A bit confused - how is not considering slippage, spread and market impact a python problem and fixed by C++? I get the speed benefits but you can get similar speed benefits by using Numpy or Numba JIT. Just use VectorBT or some other optimized library and it already supports everything you were trying to fix/do. 

If you want overkill implementation, write run simulations in PyTorch and get massive parallelized benefits running them on GPU.

comments.author: R3VO360
comments.body: Not sure why the issue you experienced with backtesting is supposed to be related to the coding language in any way. It sounds like you vibe coded something without knowing what are you talking about.
